name: override_self_resolve_transient
description: Cool day, HVAC in cool mode at comfort (74F). At 10:00 thermostat briefly reports heat mode (transient glitch). At 10:05 thermostat returns to cool before the 10-minute confirm window expires. Override self-resolves; no override is accepted; classification continues normally.
notes: Tests the 'transient glitch' vs 'real user change' distinction in the override confirmation window.
Issue #476: migrated to use_coordinator=true, skip_startup_coalesce=true, evaluated for real.

CORRECTED per #474/#476 investigation: the override_self_resolved assertion moved from 10:05 to 10:10. Tracing automation.py's start_override_confirmation()/_confirm_override_expired() shows the self-resolve check ONLY runs when the confirm timer itself fires (10:10 here — 10 minutes after detection, per override_confirm_seconds=600), reading whatever the current thermostat state is at THAT moment. There is no code path in _async_thermostat_changed that proactively cancels/re-evaluates the pending timer early when the thermostat returns to the expected mode before the window expires (confirmed by directly reading the guard chain — no such early-cancel branch exists). The scenario's original 10:05 assertion described aspirational behavior that was never implemented, not a harness dispatch-fidelity gap — do not 'fix' this by modifying automation.py/coordinator.py; that would be a production behavior change out of scope for a test-infra issue. The self-resolve genuinely happens, just at timer-expiry (10:10), not at the return-to-expected moment (10:05).

10:30's classification hvac_mode changed from 'cool' (same as 09:01) to 'off': the original, identical classification would be silently deduped by apply_classification()'s day_type/hvac_mode dedup (automation.py:1355-1356) regardless of whether the override genuinely self-resolved or incorrectly stuck — making the assertion pass vacuously either way. A different hvac_mode makes it discriminating: a genuine NEW classification_applied only fires if the override really did clear.

Revert test: temporarily forced _confirm_override_expired() (automation.py:1145) to always take PATH A (confirm) instead of checking current_mode != cls_mode — the 10:10 assertion correctly failed (override_confirmed instead of override_self_resolved). Restored; git diff confirmed clean.

events.note: Transient: thermostat briefly reports heat mode. Override detection fires; 10-min confirm window starts.
Thermostat returns to cool mode before 10:05 (transient glitch resolved)
Thermostat returns to cool mode at 10:05 — only 5 minutes elapsed, before confirm window expires. Override self-resolves.

30-min classification cycle — deliberately a DIFFERENT hvac_mode (off, not cool) from the 09:01 classification, so the assertion below genuinely discriminates (see notes). Override should NOT be active — classification should apply normally.

assertions.reason: Initial cool-day classification applied: hvac_mode=cool, target=comfort_cool=74F.
Thermostat changed to heat while classification says cool — pending override detection.
assertions.note: Override detection fires in coordinator _async_thermostat_changed listener.

assertions.reason: Confirm timer (override_confirm_seconds=600) fires at 10:10 — 10 minutes after detection at 10:00. Reads current thermostat state (still 'cool', set at 10:04:59) which matches classification.hvac_mode ('cool') — self-resolve path (PATH B in _confirm_override_expired), no override accepted. Corrected from 10:05 — see notes.
assertions.note: Self-resolve genuinely happens at real timer expiry, not proactively on return-to-expected.

assertions.reason: Override was not confirmed (self-resolved), so classification cycle at 10:30 applies normally — not suppressed.

verdict.summary: Transient thermostat state change self-resolves before confirm window; classification continues normally
verdict.expected_behavior: CA distinguishes transient thermostat glitches from real user overrides using a 10-min confirmation window
